**Upper Bavaria** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Oberbayern*, pronounced [[ˈoːbɐˌbaɪ̯ɐn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Oberbayern.ogg "File:De-Oberbayern.ogg"); [Bavarian](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language"): *Oberbayern*) is one of the seven [administrative regions](/wiki/Regierungsbezirk "Regierungsbezirk") of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Upper_Bavaria&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

Upper Bavaria is located in the southern portion of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), and is centred on the city of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), both state capital and seat of the district government. Because of this, it is by far the most populous administrative division in Bavaria. It is subdivided into four planning regions (*Planungsverband*): Ingolstadt, Munich, Bayerisches Oberland (Bavarian Highland), and Südostoberbayern (South East Upper Bavaria). The name 'Upper Bavaria' refers to the relative position on the Danube and its tributaries: downstream, Upper Bavaria is followed by [Lower Bavaria](/wiki/Lower_Bavaria "Lower Bavaria"), then [Upper Austria](/wiki/Upper_Austria "Upper Austria"), and subsequently [Lower Austria](/wiki/Lower_Austria "Lower Austria"). It consists of 20 districts and 500 municipalities (including three cities).

*Landkreise* (districts):

* [Altötting](/wiki/Alt%C3%B6tting_(district) "Altötting (district)")
* [Bad Tölz-Wolfratshausen](/wiki/Bad_T%C3%B6lz-Wolfratshausen "Bad Tölz-Wolfratshausen")

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Upper_Bavaria&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Economy")]

The gross domestic product (GDP) of the region was €273.7 billion in 2018, accounting for 8.2% of German economic output. GDP per capita adjusted for purchasing power was €53,900 or 179% of the EU27 average in the same year. The GDP per employee was 134% of the EU average. This makes Upper Bavaria one of the richest regions in Europe.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The region is served by [Munich Airport](/wiki/Munich_Airport "Munich Airport") which is also Germany's [second-busiest](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Germany "List of the busiest airports in Germany") airport after [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport") and the [eleventh-busiest in Europe](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Europe "List of the busiest airports in Europe"), handling 41.6 million passengers in 2024.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The airport had been ranked as the [39th-busiest airport worldwide](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by passenger traffic") that year.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Upper_Bavaria&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

The duchy of [Upper Bavaria](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Herzogtum_Oberbayern "de:Herzogtum Oberbayern") was created for the first time with the *First Bavarian [partition](/wiki/Partition_(politics) "Partition (politics)")* in 1255 under duke [Louis II](/wiki/Louis_II,_Duke_of_Bavaria "Louis II, Duke of Bavaria"), but there was no exact correlation between this duchy and the current territory. After the reunification in 1340 Bavaria was divided again in 1349, and in 1392 the duchies [Bavaria-Munich](/wiki/Bavaria-Munich "Bavaria-Munich") and [Bavaria-Ingolstadt](/wiki/Bavaria-Ingolstadt "Bavaria-Ingolstadt") were created in Upper Bavaria. In 1505 Bavaria was permanently reunited. For administrative purposes, Bavaria was split into *Rentämter* (plural of *[Rentamt](/w/index.php?title=Rentamt&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rentamt (page does not exist)") [[bar](https://bar.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rentamt "bar:Rentamt"); [de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rentamt "de:Rentamt"); [es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mayordom%C3%ADa "es:Mayordomía")]*). Upper Bavaria consisted of the Rentamt Munich and Rentamt Burghausen.

After the founding of the [Kingdom of Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria") the state was totally reorganised and, in 1808, divided into 15 administrative districts (Regierungsbezirke (singular Regierungsbezirk)), in Bavaria called (Kreise (singular Kreis)). They were created in the fashion of the French departements, quite even in size and population, and named after their main rivers. In the following years, due to territorial changes (e. g. loss of Tyrol, addition of the [Palatinate](/wiki/Palatinate_(region) "Palatinate (region)")), the number of districts was reduced to 8. One of these was the [Isarkreis](/wiki/Isarkreis "Isarkreis") (Isar District). In 1837 king [Ludwig I of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria") renamed the Kreise after historical names, and tribes. This also involved border changes or territorial swaps. Thus, the Isarkreis changed to Upper Bavaria.

Instead of a Rentamt-style mere administrational unit, the newly created districts became predecessors of modern regional self-government, building a political and administrational link in-between the Bavarian state as a whole and the local authorities.

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Upper_Bavaria&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Main sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Bartholomae-2005.jpg/250px-Bartholomae-2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bartholomae-2005.jpg)

Watzmann East Face, rising behind [St. Batholomew's](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Church,_Berchtesgaden "St. Bartholomew's Church, Berchtesgaden") church at lake [Königssee](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigssee "Königssee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Therme_Bad_Aibling.jpg/250px-Therme_Bad_Aibling.jpg)](/wiki/File:Therme_Bad_Aibling.jpg)

The thermal baths in [Bad Aibling](/wiki/Bad_Aibling "Bad Aibling") with its special architecture in the form of domes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Max-Josefs-Platz_Rosenheim_04.jpg/250px-Max-Josefs-Platz_Rosenheim_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Max-Josefs-Platz_Rosenheim_04.jpg)

Max Josefs Platz in the center of [Rosenheim](/wiki/Rosenheim "Rosenheim")

Featured former residence cities are the capital Munich, Ingolstadt and [Neuburg an der Donau](/wiki/Neuburg_an_der_Donau "Neuburg an der Donau") and the diocesan towns of [Freising](/wiki/Freising "Freising") and [Eichstätt](/wiki/Eichst%C3%A4tt "Eichstätt"). Interesting townscapes are found at [Landsberg am Lech](/wiki/Landsberg_am_Lech "Landsberg am Lech"), [Wasserburg am Inn](/wiki/Wasserburg_am_Inn "Wasserburg am Inn") and [Burghausen](/wiki/Burghausen,_Alt%C3%B6tting "Burghausen, Altötting") and further south [Bad Reichenhall](/wiki/Bad_Reichenhall "Bad Reichenhall") and [Berchtesgaden](/wiki/Berchtesgaden "Berchtesgaden").

The highest mountain in Upper Bavaria, [Zugspitze](/wiki/Zugspitze "Zugspitze"), offers an incomparable panoramic view of the Alps. Nestled in forested mountain ranges, the lakes [Tegernsee](/wiki/Tegernsee_(lake) "Tegernsee (lake)"), [Schliersee](/wiki/Schliersee "Schliersee"), and [Spitzingsee](/wiki/Spitzingsee "Spitzingsee"), are idyllically situated. The larger lakes, like [Starnberger See](/wiki/Starnberger_See "Starnberger See"), [Ammersee](/wiki/Ammersee "Ammersee") (south-west of Munich), and [Chiemsee](/wiki/Chiemsee "Chiemsee") further to the east, all situated in the pre-alpine uplands, offer regular Passenger services on steamers.

Sacred art treasures can be found in the monasteries [Andechs](/wiki/Andechs_Abbey "Andechs Abbey"), [Schäftlarn](/wiki/Sch%C3%A4ftlarn_Abbey "Schäftlarn Abbey"), [Fürstenfeld](/wiki/F%C3%BCrstenfeld_Abbey "Fürstenfeld Abbey"), [Benediktbeuern](/wiki/Benediktbeuern_Abbey "Benediktbeuern Abbey"), [Polling](/wiki/Polling_Abbey "Polling Abbey") and [Ettal](/wiki/Ettal_Abbey "Ettal Abbey") and in the [Wieskirche](/wiki/Wieskirche "Wieskirche"). Among popular excursions in Upper Bavaria are the [Koenigssee](/wiki/Koenigssee "Koenigssee") with the Sanctuary of St Bartholomew's and mount [Watzmann](/wiki/Watzmann "Watzmann"), the royal castles of [Ludwig II](/wiki/Ludwig_II_of_Bavaria "Ludwig II of Bavaria"), [Linderhof](/wiki/Linderhof "Linderhof") and [Herrenchiemsee](/wiki/Herrenchiemsee "Herrenchiemsee") in [Chiemsee](/wiki/Chiemsee "Chiemsee"), the [Burghausen Castle](/wiki/Burghausen_Castle "Burghausen Castle") and the castle [Hohenaschau](/wiki/Aschau_im_Chiemgau "Aschau im Chiemgau"). The most important places of pilgrimage are [Altoetting](/wiki/Altoetting "Altoetting") and [Tuntenhausen](/wiki/Tuntenhausen "Tuntenhausen").
